topic: How to create a Gmail account from scratch

inputs.topic: How to create a Gmail account from scratch

lesson.teacherObjective: After this lesson, you will be able to create a new Gmail account on an Android phone and open the inbox.
lesson.teacherExplanation: Today we are learning how to create an email account. Email is a formal way to send messages to schools, businesses, and government offices. Many jobs and forms now ask for an email address. If you do not have one, you can be left behind. Think of Gmail as a free mail box on your phone. You open the Play Store, find Gmail, and install it if needed. Then you open the app and choose to create an account. You type your real name, not a nickname, because a professional name looks serious and safe. You then choose an email address that is easy to read. Next, you set a password that is strong and hard to guess. After that, you answer a few simple questions and accept the terms. When the setup is finished, your inbox opens and you can see your new messages. In our area, Inkify uses email when they send quotes and invoices to customers. WhatsApp is still useful for quick messages, but email gives a formal record. When you use both tools well, you are ready for school, work, and small business use. Today you will practise creating your own Gmail account step by step.
teacherDiscussionQuestions: What kind of message would you send by email instead of WhatsApp?
Why is it better to use your real name in an email address?
How could a free email help a young person in a business like Inkify or a school application?
lesson.teacherLocalExample: Inkify, run by Samke Jaca and Ntokozo Gwacela, uses email to send quotes and invoices to customers. This helps them keep a formal record of what was agreed. They still use WhatsApp for quick replies, but email gives a neat written record.
teacherTimeGuide: 5 min: write board points, students copy
5 min: students start task on phones
5 min: recap — what is one thing you learned?
lesson.taskTitle: How to Create a Gmail Account
lesson.taskIntro: You will have a new Gmail account with a clean, professional email address and an open inbox.
lesson.taskReflection: You now have a working Gmail account. Write one real situation in the next few months where you will use this email address for school, work, or a customer, and explain what kind of message you will send.

steps.teach: Start on your phone home screen or app list. Open the Google Play Store so you can find Gmail and install it. If you do not see it on the first screen, swipe up to open all apps.
steps.question: What app do you open first to find Gmail?
steps.instruction: Open the Google Play Store on your phone now.
steps.visibleResult: You should see the Play Store home screen with a search bar near the top.
steps.feedbackCorrect: Good — the Google Play Store is the right place to find the Gmail app.
steps.feedbackWrong: Go back to your home screen or app list and look for the Play Store triangle icon.
steps.tip: If your phone asks you to sign in first, complete that screen and then return to this task.

steps.teach: At the top of the Play Store, you will see a search bar. You will tap it and type Gmail so the store can find the correct app. Check your spelling before you continue.
steps.question: What word did you type in the search bar?
steps.instruction: Tap the search bar at the top of the Play Store and type Gmail.
steps.visibleResult: You should see search results related to Gmail on your screen.
steps.feedbackCorrect: Correct — typing Gmail brings up the app you need.
steps.feedbackWrong: Look at the top search bar in the Play Store and check the exact word you entered.
steps.tip: Do not type 'G mail' with a space.

steps.teach: The search results show different apps. Tap the Gmail result to open its app detail page and check that it is the real Gmail app. This helps you avoid opening the wrong app.
steps.question: Which app result should you tap to open the correct app page?
steps.feedbackCorrect: Yes — Gmail is the correct app result to open.
steps.feedbackWrong: Look at the app names in the search results and choose the one called Gmail.
steps.tip: Check the app name carefully before you tap.

steps.teach: On the Gmail app page, you will see a button that downloads the app to your phone. Tap Install and wait for it to finish. If your phone is slow, give it a little time before trying again.
steps.question: Which button starts the download of Gmail?
steps.instruction: Tap Install on the Gmail app page and wait for the app to download and install.
steps.visibleResult: You should see the button change while the app downloads. After it finishes, the button changes to Open.
steps.feedbackCorrect: Right — Install is the button that puts Gmail on your phone.
steps.feedbackWrong: Look at the main button on the Gmail app page, under the app name and icon.
steps.tip: If you already see Open, Gmail is already installed and you can move to the next step.

steps.teach: When the install finishes, the button changes from Install to Open. Tap Open to launch Gmail for the first time. This takes you out of the Play Store and into the Gmail app.
steps.question: What button appears after Gmail finishes installing?
steps.instruction: Tap Open on the Gmail app page to launch Gmail.
steps.visibleResult: You should see the Gmail app open on a welcome screen.
steps.feedbackCorrect: Correct — Open launches Gmail after the install is complete.
steps.feedbackWrong: Stay on the Gmail app page and wait until the download finishes, then check the main button again.
steps.tip: Do not close the Play Store before the install is complete.

steps.teach: The first Gmail screen usually says Welcome to Gmail. This is where you start making your new account. Look for the button that lets you begin the setup.
steps.question: On the Welcome to Gmail screen, what button do you tap to begin a new account?
steps.feedbackCorrect: Good — Create account is the button that starts a new Gmail account.
steps.feedbackWrong: Look near the bottom of the Welcome to Gmail screen for the button that starts setup.
steps.tip: Do not tap Sign in if you do not already have a Google account.

steps.teach: Gmail now asks what kind of account you want to create. For this lesson, choose the option for your own personal use. This is the correct path for a first Gmail account.
steps.question: Which option should you tap on the account type screen?
steps.feedbackCorrect: Yes — For myself is the correct option for your own new Gmail account.
steps.feedbackWrong: Look at the list of choices and pick the option that is for your own use.
steps.tip: You can still use this email for school, work, or business messages later.

steps.teach: You will now type your first name and last name. Use your real name, not a nickname, because a clean name looks more professional to a school, employer, or customer. Then tap Next.
steps.question: After typing your first name and last name, which button do you tap?
steps.instruction: Type your First name and Last name into the two boxes, then tap Next.
steps.visibleResult: You should move to the next screen after your names are entered.
steps.feedbackCorrect: Correct — Next takes you forward after entering your real name.
steps.feedbackWrong: Look below the name fields for the button that moves you to the next screen.
steps.tip: Do not use a nickname like 'coolboy' or 'queen123' in your name fields.

steps.teach: Google asks for your date of birth and gender on the next screen. Fill in each field carefully, then tap Next. If the screen uses drop-down boxes, tap each box to choose the right details.
steps.question: Match each field to what you enter there:

pairs.match: Part of your date of birth
Number of the day you were born

steps.feedbackCorrect: Well done — you know what each field on the basic information screen means.
steps.feedbackWrong: Check each field name on the screen and think about what information belongs there.
steps.tip: Read the month, day, and year boxes carefully so you do not swap them.

steps.teach: Now you choose your Gmail address, also called your username. Try a clean version of your real name first, like your name and surname. If the name is taken, Gmail shows suggestions you can choose from.
steps.question: If the Gmail address you want is already taken, what should you do next?
options: Choose one of the suggestions or try another name
steps.correctAnswer: Choose one of the suggestions or try another name
steps.instruction: Type a username for your new Gmail address, check the suggestions if needed, and tap Next.
steps.visibleResult: You should move forward if the username is available. If not, Gmail shows suggestions or asks you to try another one.
steps.feedbackCorrect: Yes — if a username is taken, Gmail helps you with suggestions or lets you try another one.
steps.feedbackWrong: Look on the username screen for suggested addresses under the name you typed.
steps.tip: Avoid unprofessional names like hotboy99 or princessqueen if you want to use this email for jobs or customers.

steps.teach: Your password protects your new account. Type a strong password, then type the same password again in Confirm. Use at least eight characters and include letters and numbers if possible.
steps.question: The second box on this screen asks you to type your password again. What is that box called?
steps.feedbackCorrect: Correct — Confirm is where you type the same password again.
steps.feedbackWrong: Look at the label above the second password box on the screen.
steps.tip: Write your password somewhere safe because you will need it again later.

steps.teach: Some phones now show a screen asking for your phone number. This helps with security and can help you get back into your account later. If you see this screen, type your number and tap Next.
steps.question: Why does Gmail ask for your phone number on this screen?
options: To make your account safer and help with recovery
steps.correctAnswer: To make your account safer and help with recovery
steps.instruction: If Gmail asks for your phone number, type it and tap Next.
steps.visibleResult: You should move to a screen for SMS verification, or continue if your phone skips this step.
steps.feedbackCorrect: Good — the phone number step helps protect your account and recover it later.
steps.feedbackWrong: Read the small text on the phone number screen about security and recovery.
steps.tip: If this screen does not appear on your phone, move to the next step.

steps.teach: If Gmail sends an SMS code, you must type that code on the verification screen. This proves that the phone number belongs to you. Enter the numbers carefully, then tap Verify.
steps.question: Arrange these actions in the correct order for phone verification:
steps.feedbackCorrect: Exactly — that is the correct order for verifying your phone number.
steps.feedbackWrong: Think about what must happen before you can type the code.
steps.tip: If the SMS does not arrive, wait a short time and check your signal.

steps.teach: Before your account is created, Gmail shows Privacy and Terms. Scroll if needed, then tap the button that accepts the terms. This is the final permission step before setup finishes.
steps.question: Which button accepts the Privacy and Terms and lets setup continue?
steps.feedbackCorrect: Yes — I Agree accepts the Privacy and Terms and continues setup.
steps.feedbackWrong: Look near the bottom of the Privacy and Terms screen for the button that accepts the terms.
steps.tip: You may need to scroll down before the button is easy to see.

steps.teach: Gmail now finishes the setup and opens your inbox. This means your new Gmail account has been created. You can now receive messages like Inkify does when sending quotes and invoices to customers.
steps.question: What screen shows that your Gmail account is ready to use?
steps.instruction: Wait for Gmail to finish setup and check that the inbox opens.
steps.visibleResult: You should see the Gmail inbox screen with your new account ready to use.
steps.feedbackCorrect: Correct — the inbox screen shows that your Gmail account is created and open.
steps.feedbackWrong: Wait a moment for Gmail to finish loading, then look for the screen that lists your emails.
steps.tip: If Gmail stays blank, close the app and open it again.

lesson.teacherPlanHTML:
Learning Objective

Create a Gmail Account

After this lesson, you will be able to create a new Gmail account on an Android phone and open the inbox.

✏ Write on the Board
Write these before class starts — students copy them down
  • Email is formal messaging
  • Use your real name
  • Choose a clear address
  • Set a strong password
  • Check your inbox

2Explain to Students

Read this aloud — then explain in your own words

Today we are learning how to create an email <span class="hover-word" data-zu="i-akhawunti">account</span>. Email is a formal way to send messages to schools, businesses, and government offices. Many jobs and forms now ask for an email address. If you do not have one, you can be left behind.

Think of Gmail as a free mail box on your phone. You open the Play Store, find Gmail, and install it if needed. Then you open the app and choose to create an account. You type your real name, not a nickname, because a <span class="hover-word" data-zu="umuntu oyingcweti">professional</span> name looks serious and safe. You then choose an email address that is easy to read. Next, you set a password that is strong and hard to guess. After that, you answer a few simple questions and accept the terms. When the setup is finished, your inbox opens and you can see your new messages.

In our area, Inkify uses email when they send quotes and <span class="hover-word" data-zu="i-invoice">invoice</span>s to customers. WhatsApp is still useful for quick messages, but email gives a formal record. When you use both tools well, you are ready for school, work, and small business use. Today you will practise creating your own Gmail account step by step.

3Discussion Questions

  1. 1What kind of message would you send by email instead of WhatsApp?
  2. 2Why is it better to use your real name in an email address?
  3. 3How could a free email help a young person in a business like Inkify or a school application?

4Local Example

Inkify, run by Samke Jaca and Ntokozo Gwacela, uses email to send quotes and invoices to customers. This helps them keep a formal record of what was agreed. They still use WhatsApp for quick replies, but email gives a neat written record.
Time Guide — 30 minutes
5 minwrite board points, students copy
12 minteacher explains
8 minclass discussion
5 minstudents start task on phones
5 minrecap — what is one thing you learned?

improvementNote: The steps are good and interactive. It should recognise if you made the step or not.
